Facts.text: The first Christmas according to calendar-maker and Eastern Roman monk Dionysius Exiguus
Earliest possible date that Christmas was celebrated on Dec 25th
1st definite date Christmas was celebrated on Dec 25th
Roman emperor Theodosius admits debt on mass murder in Thessalonica
King Clovis I is baptized a Roman Catholic in Reims cathedral - first Germanic king to do so, according to Henry of Tours (year disputed)
England adopts Julian calendar
Battle at Etampes (Stampae): Burgundy beat Neustriers
Charles the Bald crowned emperor of Rome
John XIII crowned Otto II the Red German compassionate emperor
Johannes I Tzimisces crowned Emperor of Byzantium
Heribertus becomes bishop of Cologne
Monarch Istvan crowned king of Hungary
English earls declare Danish king Sweyn Forkbeard the first Viking king of England, disposing Ethelred the Unready
Pope Clement II crowns Henry III as Holy Roman Emperor
Parliament of Worms: Emperor Henry III names his cousin Count Bruno van Egisheim-Dagsburg as Pope Leo IX
Boudouin I of Boulogne crowned King of Jerusalem
Henry I of Limburg becomes Duke of Lower Lorraine
Norbert of Xanten, later St. Norbert, founds the Premonstratensian Order at Prémontré in northern France
Holy Roman Emperor Henry VI crowned King of Sicily in Palermo Cathedral
John IV Lascaris of the restored Eastern Roman Empire is deposed and blinded by orders of his co-ruler Michael VIII Palaeologus.
Ottoman troops occupy the island of Rhodes after a six-month siege
Battle of Tucapel: Mapuche rebels under Lautaro defeats the Spanish conquistadors and executes the governor of Chile Pedro de Valdivia
The city of Natal, Brazil, is founded.
Johan Sigismund of Brandenburg becomes protestant
Emperor Ferdinand III makes appointments with Sweden and France
Christmas Island founded and named by Captain William Mynors of the East India Ship Company vessel, the Royal Mary.
Massachusetts General Court ordered a five shilling fine for "observing any such day as Christmas"
English Whig leader Duke of Monmouth flees to Holland
Floods ravage Dutch coast provinces, thousands killed
Prussia and Austria sign Treaty of Dresden giving much of Silesia to the Prussians
Return of Halley's comet 1st sighted by Johann Georg Palitzsch
Jupiter Hammon, an African American slave, composes poetry broadside "An Evening Thought" (1st poetry published by an African American 1761)
First Christian service in New Zealand; Mass is celebrated in Doubtless Bay by Father Paul-Antoine Léonard de Villefeix of the de Surville expedition
Physician Ephraim McDowell performs the first abdominal surgery in the U.S, an ovariotomy to remove a 22 lb ovarian tumor
Reverend Samuel Marsden of the Church Missionary Society holds the first Christian service in New Zealand on land, at Rangihoua
First known Christmas carol, "Silent Night, Holy Night" (Stille Nacht, Heilige Nacht'), is sung in Austria
Handel's "Messiah", premieres in the US in Boston, performed by The Handel & Haydn Society
Louisiana & Arkansas are first states to observe Christmas as holiday
Battle of Okeechobee - US forces defeat Seminole Indians
1st theatre matinee (Olympic Theatre, NYC)
New Haven Railroad opens
William & Ellen Craft escape from slavery in Georgia, arriving in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania; later move to Boston where they become abolitionists
40,000 watch Union army men play baseball at Hilton Head, South Carolina
Lambs Club in New York forms
First indoor baseball game played at fairgrounds in Philadelphia; 2,000 watch the Uptowners beat the Downtowners, 6-1
1st midwestern football team to play on west coast, University of Chicago defeats Stanford 24-4 at Palo Alto, CA in football
Shelling on besieged Ladysmith during the Boer War includes one shell containing a plum pudding
Arthur Schnitzler's novel "Leutnant Gustl" ("None But The Brave") banned in Germany due to its unflattering portrait of military protocol
Battle at Tweefontein: British force camped on a hill is surprised by a Boer attack at 2am
Edward Knoblock's "Kismet" premieres in NYC
Legendary "Christmas Truce" takes place on the battlefields of WWI between British and German troops. Instead of fighting, soldiers exchange gifts and play football
"Why Marry" 1st drama to win Pulitzer Prize, premieres in NYC
Last major Potlatch (ceremony) by Chief Daniel Cranmer (Kwakwaka’wakw) from Alert Bay, British Columbia. Twenty-six people arrested, their masks and regalia sold by Canadian government.
Imperial Theater opens at 249 W 45th St NYC
NSW (v Vic) go from 8-74 to 9-113 to be 9-367 at stumps
Mt, Van Hoevenberg bobsled run at Lake Placid, NY opens; first American bobsled run open to public
Slinger Nitschke scores 142 SA v Qld at Adelaide before 5,422
Tasmania all out 280, WI 2-139 at Hobart Crowd 2,500
Albert Lonergan scores 137 SA v Qld at Adelaide before 5,697
Fleetwood-Smith takes 5-69 Victoria v Tasmania at Hobart
New York's Metropolitan Opera broadcasts an entire live opera performance for the first time - Englebert Humperdinck's "Hänsel und Gretel" over NBC radio
7.6 magnitude earthquake ravages Qansu, China, kills 275
Another Christmas Day five-wicket haul byClarrie Grimmett
Belgian Working people's party accept Henry de Mans Plan of Labor
Stan Smith takes 8-33 for Victoria v Tasmania at Hobart
Four centuries for SA as they make 7-644 v Qld before 6,180
Samson Raphaelson's "Accent on Youth" premieres in NYC
Belgian bishops condemn fascism & communism
Ron Hamence scores 104 for SA v Queensland before 4,865
Queensland all out for 93 v SA in front of 10,436
Clarrie Grimmett& Ward rip through Qld except Bill Brown (156)
Montgomery Ward introduces Rudolph the 9th red-nosed reindeer
Japan announces surrender of British-Canadian garrison at Hong Kong
Japanese aircraft carriers Akagi/Kagu back in Kure, Japan
Admiral Dalans murderer of Bosinier de la Chapelle, sentenced to death
British Colonel S W Bailey reaches Mihailovics headquarter
Soviet artillery and tank battle on German armies at Stalingrad
Constitution accepted in Taiwan
Constitution of the Republic of China comes into effect
Taiwan passes Human Rights laws (Day of Earth Law)
Coronation Stone, taken from Scone in Scotland by Edward I in 1296, stolen from Westminster Abbey & smuggled back to Scotland
West Indies wrap up 6 wicket defeat of Australia on first Christmas Day of Test cricket, the 3rd day of 3rd Test at the Adelaide Oval
Avalanche of lava kills 150 from Ruapehu volcano in New Zealand
KNEV (95.5 The Vibe) radio broadcaster in Reno, Nevada begins broadcasting
WSFA TV channel 12 in Montgomery, AL (NBC) begins broadcasting
A synagogue in Cologne Germany desecrated with swatstikas
Sony brings transistor TV 8-301 to the market
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya USSR
Walt Disney's "The Sword in the Stone" is released
The Yemeni Nasserite Unionist People's Organisation is founded in Taiz
42 Dalits are burned alive in Kilavenmani village, Tamil Nadu, India, a retaliation for a campaign for higher wages by Dalit labourers
5 Israeli gunboats escape from Cherbourg harbor
India all out for 163 at Madras v Aust, Ashley Mallett 5-91
Longest game in NFL history as Miami Dolphins beat KC Chiefs, 27-24 in 2OT in AFL playoff game; duration: 82 minutes and 40 seconds
Worst hotel fire in history kills 163 at Taeyokale Hotel in Seoul
1st pictures of a comet from space (Comet Kohoutek), taken by NASA astronauts Jerry Carr and William Pogue during a spacewalk from Skylab 4
Arab oil ministers cancel January 5 percent production cut; Saudi Arabian oil minister promises 10 percent OPEC production rise
The ARPANET crashes when a programming bug causes all ARPANET traffic to be routed through the server at Harvard University, causing the server to freeze
Tommy Chambers (Scotland) finishes 51 yr cycle tour (799,405 miles)
Cyclone Tracy virtually destroys Darwin, Australia
Marshall Fields drives a vehicle through the gates of the White House, resulting in a four-hour standoff.
Egyptian SS Patria sinks in Red Sea, about 100 killed
Takeo Fukuda becomes Japanese premier
Ballon d'Or: Hamburg's English forward Kevin Keegan wins his 2nd consecutive trophy as best football player in Europe; beats Bayern Munich forward Karl-Heinz Rummenigge and Ajax sweeper Ruud Krol
Soviet forces invade Afghanistan to prop up the Communist government, beginning a disastrous and failed ten-year war
1st live telecast of Christmas Parade at the EPCOT Centre, Disney World Florida
Knicks basketball forward Bernard King scores 60 points, but New York loses, 120-114 to the New Jersey Nets on Xmas Day at Madison Square Garden
Lynette "Squeaky" Fromme, who escaped 2 days earlier, recaptured
Japanese scientist achieve -271.8°C, coldest temperature ever recorded
Ballon d'Or: Inter's German sweeper Lothar Matthäus is named best football player in Europe ahead of Juventus striker Salvatore Schillaci and Inter defender Andreas Brehme
Last day of a washout Pakistan v Sri Lanka at Gujranwala
1,500 year anniversary of Catholicism in France commemorating the baptism of Clovis I in Rheims
For 1st time US movie box office receipts pass $6 billion
PopeJohn Paul IIpersonally opens the Holy Doors of St. John Lateran in Rome to mark the Jubilee
The ill-fated Beagle 2 probe which was released from the Mars Express Spacecraft on December 19, disappears shortly before its scheduled landing.
Cassini orbiter releases Huygens probe which successfully landed on Saturn's moon Titan on January 14, 2005.
Umar Farouk Abdulmutallab unsuccessfully attempts a terrorist attack against the US while on board Northwest Airlines Flight 253
27 people are killed after an Antonov An-72 plane crashes near Shymkent, Kazakhstan
8 people are killed and thousands left homeless after two fires strike Manila, Philippines
Russian military defence plane crashes into the Black Sea after takeoff from Sochi airport, killing all 92 on board

An RV detonates in downtown Nashville, Tennessee causing damage to people and property; prior to detonating, a recorded message playing from speakers warned anyone in earshot to evacuate prior to an explosion
Historical romance "Bridgerton" debuts on Netflix based on the books by Julia Quinn, starring Phoebe Dynevor and Regé-Jean Page in Season One
New Orleans running back Alvin Kamara equals Ernie Nevers 1929 NFL record of 6 touchdowns in a match in the Saints' 52-33 win over the Minnesota Vikings at the Mercedes-Benz Superdome
NASA's James Webb Space Telescope launched in joint effort with ESA (European Space Agency) and the Canadian Space Agency on an Ariane 5 rocket from Europe’s Spaceport in French Guiana, South America
Rare polar rain aurora, stretching for 3,000 km, is visible across the North Pole, caused by charged particles flowing from the Sun
Ncuti Gatwa makes his debut as the fifteenth incarnation of the Doctor in "Doctor Who", with Millie Gibson as his companion
Azerbaijani airliner crashes near Kazakhstani city of Aktau, killing 38 people, mistakenly shot down by Russian air defences
Roman EmperorAureliandedicates a temple to Sol Invictus in Rome, on supposed day of the winter solstice, fulfilling vow made during campaign in Palmyra
Pope Leo III crowns Charles the Great (Charlemagne), Roman Emperor
Anti-pope Anacletus II crownsRoger IIthe Norman king of Sicily
StFrancis of Assisiassembles 1st Nativity scene (Greccio, Italy)
English KingEdward IVholds a lavish Christmas feast for 2,000 people at Eltham Palace
French Huguenots declare their intention to create a "state within the state" at a gathering in La Rochelle
GovernorWilliam Bradfordof Plymouth Colony (now in Massachusetts) forbids game playing on Christmas Day
Pierre de Fermatwrites to Marin Mersenne about Fermat's church thesis
Dutch astronomer and mathematicianChristiaan Huygenscreates the first pendulum clock
British KingJames IIlands in Ambleteuse, France
St Paul's Cathedral, designed by SirChristopher Wren, is officially declared complete after 35 years construction, by the British parliament
AstronomerAnders Celsiusintroduces Centigrade temperature scale
PopePius VIencyclical on the problems of the pontificate
American Revolutionary War:George Washingtoncrosses the Delaware River into New Jersey, surprises and defeats 1,400 Hessians
Despite bitter opposition, US PresidentAndrew Johnsongrants unconditional pardon to all persons involved in Southern rebellion (Civil War)
Patriotic march "Stars & Stripes Forever" is written byJohn Philip Sousa
Clyde Fitch' play "The Girl with the Green Eyes" premieres in NYC
PopeLeo XIII, at his annual Christmas reception, endorses the Christian Democratic movement now emerging in Europe as an attempt to offer an alternative to more radical movements
"Lenin's testament" -Vladimir Lenindictates document criticizing his colleagues, suggestsStalinbe removed as General Secretary (until Dec 26)
Crown PrinceHirohitoof Japan assumes the throne upon his father, Emperor Yoshihito's, death
Clarrie Grimmetttakes 6-146 for SA, Queensland all out 380 Crowd 5,390
During KingGeorge V's Christmas dinner speech his chair collapses
Arturo Toscaniniconducts the first "Symphony of the Air" concert program over NBC radio
George CukorannouncesVivien Leighwill play Scarlett O'Hara in "Gone With The Wind"
First television adaptation ofCharles Dickens' "A Christmas Carol" debuts in American syndication; low budget 30-minute production stars Taylor Holmes and is narrated by Vincent Price
PopePius XIIencyclical "Musice Sacrae" on the history, use, and promotion of sacred music is published

Detroit's future Hockey Hall of Fame right wingGordie Howepicks up a Xmas hat-trick & 3 assists in Red Wings' 8-1 win over NY Rangers; most points he scores in a single game in entire 26-year NHL career
17-year old Liverpudlian Richard Starkey (later known asRingo Starr) receives his first drum set as a Christmas gift from his stepfather
AmericanEd Geinfound not guilty by reason of insanity for a series of murders in Plainsfield, Wisconsin
Bishop Karol Wojtyla (future PopeJohn Paul II) celebrates outdoor Mass at midnight of Christmas morning to demonstrate the need for a Catholic church in the community of Nowa Huta
"To Kill a Mockingbird", a film adaptation of the novel byHarper Lee, directed by Robert Mulligan and starringGregory Peck, is released (Gregory Peck - Best Actor Academy Awards 1963)
Beatle guitaristGeorge Harrison's girlfriendPattie Boydattacked by female Beatle fans
US PresidentLyndon B. Johnsonorders a halt to bombing operations in North Vietnam, hoping to spur peace talks
BeatlePaul McCartneyand British actress Jane Asher get engaged; engagement publicly broken six months later
NASA Apollo 8 crew broadcast while orbiting the moon amd read passages from the Bible to celebrate Christmas
"The Sting" directed by George Roy Hill, starringPaul NewmanandRobert Redford, with music inspired byScott Joplin, premieres in Los Angeles and New York (Best Picture 1974)
Ballon d'Or: Ajax forwardJohan Cruyffwins his second award for best European football player ahead of Juventus goalkeeper Dino Zoff and Bayern Munich strikerGerd Müller
Israeli Prime MinisterMenachem Beginmeets Egyptian PresidentAnwar Sadatin Egypt
Ballon d'Or: Juventus' French midfielderMichel Platiniis named Europe's best football player for the 2nd consecutive time; beats Bordeaux midfielder Jean Tigana and Verona striker Preben Elkjær
Trial of Romanian Communist dictator Nicolae Ceaușescu and his wife Elena on charges of genocide and personal enrichment; the couple are found guilty and executed by firing squad the same day.
"TheGodfatherPart III" directed byFrancis Ford Coppolaand starringAl Pacino,Diane Keatonand Andy Garcia premieres
Mikhail Gorbachevformally resigns as President of USSR in a televised speech
Dallas Cowboys running backEmmitt Smithscores NFL record 25th touchdown of the season in 37-13 win against Arizona Cardinals at Sun Devil Stadium, Tempe AZ
Jerry Seinfeldannounces the final season of his TV show "Seinfeld" will air the following year
Phil Jacksonbecomes the sixth coach in NBA history to win 1,000 games as his LA Lakers beat Boston Celtics, 92-83 at the Staples Center, Los Angeles
Chinese human rights activistLiu Xiaobosentenced to 11 years in prison in Beijing, China for "inciting subversion of state power"
"The Wolf of Wall Street", starringLeonardo DiCaprioandJonah Hill, is released
"Unbroken", drama film directed byAngelina Jolieand starring Jack O'Connell and Domhall Gleeson, released in the United States
Baltimore RavensLamar Jacksonbreaks NFL career rushing record for quarterbacks in 31-2 win over the Texans in Houston; he runs for 87 yards, pushing his career rushing total to 6,110
William the Conqueroris crowned King of England at Westminster Abbey, completing the Norman conquest of England
Christopher Columbus' flagship the Santa María runs aground and sinks on the north coast of Hispaniola. The crew are left to found a colony as Columbus returns to Spain
Zealand and Brabant adopts Gregorian calendar, yesterday was Dec 14th

Queen Elizabeth IImakes her first Christmas Day broadcast, thanking her subjects for their "loyalty and affection" and promising to carry on the tradition, which she does every year of her reign except 1969
Leonard Bernsteinconducts Beethoven's Symphony No. 9 in East Berlin's Schauspielhaus to celebrate fall of the Berlin Wall. Broadcast worldwide to audience of 100 million.
John IV Doukas Laskaris, 4th emperor of the Nicaean Empire (1258-61), born in Nicaea (d. circa 1305)
Christina of Saxony, Queen of Denmark, Norway and Sweden, born in Torgau, Saxony (d. 1521)
Christine of Saxony, German noblewoman and landgravine consort of Hesse by marriage to Philip I, born in Dresden, Germany (d. 1549)
Orlando Gibbons, English composer (O Clap Your Hands), baptized in Oxford, England (d. 1625)
Margarita of Austria, Queen of Spain and Portugal by marriage to King Philip III & II of Spain, born in Graz, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1611)
Jose Ximenez, Spanish composer, born in Zaragoza, Spain (d. 1672)
Angelus Silesius [Johann Scheffler], German mystic (St Seelenlust), born in Breslau, the capital of Silesia (d. 1677)
Noel Coypel, French painter, born in Paris, France (d. 1707)
Harald Vallerius, Swedish composer and teacher (1697 choral book), born in pVallerstad parish, Östergötland, Sweden (d. 1716)
Archibald Pitcairne, Scottish physician and poet, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1713)
Lady Grizel Baillie (neé Hume), Scottish poet and songwriter, born at Redbraes Castle, Berwickshire, Scotland (d. 1746)
Melusine von der Schulenburg, Duchess of Kendal, German royal mistress to King George I of Great Britain, born in Emden, Germany (d. 1743)
Thomas Halyburton, Scottish "divine", an Anglican clergy of the Church of England, born in Perth, Scotland (d. 1712)
Giovanni Battista Somis, Italian Baroque violinist and composer, born in Turin, Duchy of Savoy (d. 1673)
Jacobus Houbraken, Dutch engraver and illustrator, born in Dordrecht, (d. 1789)
Leopold II of Anhalt-Dessau, German prince of the House of Ascania and Prussian general, born in Dessau (d. 1751)
Jean-Joseph Cassenea de Mondonville, French composer and violinist, born in Narbonne, France (d. 1772)
Johann Jakob Reiske, German scholar and physician, born in Zörbig, Saxony (d. 1774)

George Augustus Eliott, 1st Baron Heathfield, Scottish general (Seven Years' War) and Governor of Gibraltar (1777-90) who commanded the garrison during the Great Siege Gibraltar, born in Wells House, Roxburghshire, Scotland (d. 1790)
William Collins, English poet (Ode to Simplicity), born in Chichester England, (d. 1759)
Esteban Salas y Castro, Cuban composer, born in Havana, Cuba (d. 1803)
Johann Adam Hiller, German composer, born in Wendisch-Ossig, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1804)
Philip Mazzei, Italian physician, philosopher, and friend of Thomas Jefferson, born in Poggio a Caiano, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1816)
Charlotte von Stein, German poet known for her affair with Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, born in Eisenach, Saxe-Weimar (d. 1827)
Joseph Bologne, French violinist, conductor, composer, and champion fencer, born in Baillif, Guadeloupe, French West Indies (d. 1799)
John Christmas Beckwith, British organist and composer (The First Verse of Every Psalm of David with an Ancient or Modern Chant), born in Norwich, England (d. 1809)
Benjamin Pierce, American politician and governor for New Hampshire (1827-28; 1829-30), born in Chelmsford, Massachusetts (d. 1839)
Claude Chappe, French engineer (optical telegraph), born in Brûlon, France (d. 1805)
Joseph Mazzinghi, English composer, born in London, England (d. 1844)
Dorothy Wordsworth, English poet, diarist and sister of William Wordsworth, born in Cockermouth, Cumberland, England (d. 1855)
Antun Sorkocevic, Ragusan composer and diplomat, born in Dubrovnik, Republic of Ragusa (d. 1841)
Stephen Cleeg Rowan, American Navy Admiral during the Mexican-American War and the Civil War, born near Dublin, Ireland (d. 1890)
Wilhelm Emmanuel von Ketteler, German politician and bishop of Mainz, born in Münster, Westphalia (d. 1877)
Milledge Luke Bonham, American politician, Governor of South Carolina (1862-64) and Confederate general, born near Redbank, South Carolina (d. 1890)
Jan de Liefde II, Dutch vicar (set up For the Welfare of the People organization), born in Amsterdam (d. 1869)
Temistocle Solera, Italian opera composer, born in Ferrara, Austrian Empire (d. 1878)
Preston Smith, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Giles County, Tennessee (d. 1863)
Patrick Gilmore, Irish-born American bandmaster and composer (When Johnny Comes Marching Home), born in Ballygar, Ireland (d. 1892)
Johann Herbeck, Austrian composer and conductor (conducted premiere of Franz Schubert's "Unfinished" Symphony"), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1877)
Thomas Alfred Smyth, Irish-American Major General (Union Army), born in County Cork, Republic of Ireland (d. 1865)
Cosima Liszt, Austrian wife of composer Richard Wagner (founded Bayreuth Festival together), daughter of Franz Liszt and Marie d'Agoult, born in Bellagio, Lombardy, Austrian Empire (d. 1930)
Salvatore Auteri-Manzocchi, Italian opera composer (Graziella), born in Palermo, Kingdome of the Two Sicilies (d. 1924)
Hans von Bartels, German painter (Three Dutch Fisher Girls), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1913)
James "Pud" Galvin, American Baseball HOF pitcher (MLB's first 300-game winner; no-hitters 1880, 84; Buffalo Bisons), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1902)
Raoul Gunsbourg, French composer, impresario and opera director (Opéra de Monte-Carlo, 1892-1951), born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 1955)
Manwel Dimech, Maltese philosopher and social reformer who advocated for Malta to become a republic, born in Valletta, Malta (d. 1921)
Madan Mohan Malaviya, Indian co-founder of Banaras Hindu University, born in Allahabad, British India (d. 1946)
Charles Pathé, French pioneer of film and record industries (Pathé Brothers, 1896), born in Paris, France (d. 1957)
Thomas Cahill, American soccer coach and administrator (first coach US men's national team), born in New York City (d. 1951)
Evangeline Cory Booth, English-American religious leader (Salvation Army general 1904-34), born in London, England (d. 1950)
Alfred Kerr [Kempner], German-British writer, born in Breslau, Silesia (d. 1948)
Eugenie Besserer, American actress (Jazz Singer), born in Watertown, New York (d. 1934)
Helena Rubinstein, Polish-born American cosmetics manufacturer and art collector, born in Kraków, Poland (d. 1965)
Otto Frederick Hunziker, Swiss-American dairy industry pioneer (involved in the American Dairy Science Association), born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1959)
Lina Cavalieri, Italian dramatic soprano, and silent film actress, born in Viterbo, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1944)
Theodor Innitzer, Austrian cardinal and archbishop Vienna, born in Neugeschrei, Bohemia (d. 1955)
Walter Lees, English cricket fast bowler (5 Tests, 26 wickets), born in Sowerby Bridge, Yorkshire, England (d. 1924)
Adolf Windaus, German chemist and winner of the Nobel Prize in Chemistry 1928, born in Berlin, German Empire (d. 1959)[1]
William Starling Burgess, American yacht designer (America's Cup yachts "Enterprise", "Rainbow", "Ranger") and aviation pioneer, born in Boston Massachusetts (d. 1947)
Fran Lhotka, Croatian composer (The Devil in The Village), conductor, and educator, born in Mladá Vožice, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1962)
Maurice Utrillo, French painter (Port St Martin, Montmartre), born in Montmartre, Paris, France (d. 1955)
Evelyn Nesbit, American 'Gibson Girl' model, actress and wife of murderer Harry Kendall Thaw, born in Tarentum, Pennsylvania (d. 1967)
Edward "Kid" Ory, American New Orleans style jazz trombonist, and band leader, born in LaPlace, Louisiana (d. 1973)
Conrad Hilton, American hotel mogul (Hilton Hotels, world's 1st international hotel chain), born in san Antonio, New Mexico (d. 1979)
John Davidson, American actor (Dick Tracy vs Crime Inc, Charlie Chan-Chinese Cat), born in New York City (d. 1968)
Robert Ripley, collector of odd facts (Believe it or Not), born in Santa Rosa, California (d. 1949)
Earle Foxe, American actor (Dance Fools Dance), born in Oxford, Ohio (d. 1973)
Kenneth Anderson, British general (Operation Torch - North Africa), born in Madras, British India (d. 1959)
Rebecca West [Cicely Isabel Fairfield], English author (Meaning of Treason), born in London (d. 1983)
Bert Bertram, Australian actor (How to Steal a Million, Shamus), born in Peterborough, Australia (d. 1991)
Fred Hillebrand, American actor (Martin Kane, Moon Over Manhattan), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1963)
Theo Swagemakers, Dutch portrait painter (Queen Juliana, Princess Beatrix), born in Tilburg, Netherlands (d. 1994)[1]
Raphael Soyer, Russian-born american artist (Depression Scenes in NYC), born in Borisoglebsk, Tambov (d. 1987)
Gladys Swarthout, American mezzo-soprano (La Gioconda), born in Deepwater, Missouri (d. 1969)
W L "Tich" Cornford, English cricket wicketkeeper (4 Tests, 8 dismissals; Sussex CCC), born in Hurst Green, England (d. 1964)
Alice, English princess and Duchess of Gloucester, aunt of Elizabeth II, born in London, England (d. 2004)
Barton Maclane, American actor (The Quarterback, I Dream of Jeannie), born in Columbia, South Carolina (d. 1969)
Antiochos Evanghelatos, Greek classical composer, born in Lixouri, Cefalonia, Greece (d. 1981)
Corry Lievens [Isabella MJ Adriaens], Flemish actress (Blue Bird), born in Antwerpen, Flanders, Belgium (d. 1968)
J Edward Bromberg, Hungarian actor (The Mark of Zorro, Under 2 Flags), born in Temesvár, Austria-Hungary (d. 1951)
Gerhard Herzberg, German-Canadian physicist (molecular structure-Nobel 1971), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1999)
Ann Ronell [Rosenblatt], American songwriter ("Willow Weep for Me", "Who's Afraid of the Big Bad Wolf"), and film score composer, born in Omaha, Nebraska (d. 1993)
Clark M. Clifford, American lawyer and presidential advisor (US Secretary of Defense 1968-69), born in Fort Scott, Kansas (d. 1998)
Ernst Ruska, German physicist and winner of the Nobel Prize in Physics 1986, born in Heidelberg, German Empire (d. 1988)[1]
Frank Ferguson, American character actor (My Friend Flicka - "Gus"; Peyton Place - "Eli Carson"), born in Ferndale, California (d. 1978) [note some sources give year as 1899]
Herman Jan Scheltema [NEM Pareau], Dutch jurist and poet, born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1981)
Lew Grade, British TV mogul (ATV), movie producer (Boys from Brazil), born in Tokmak, Uyezd, Taurida Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1998)
William McChesney Martin, American Chairman of Federal Reserve Bank (1951-70), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1998)
Andrew Cruickshank, Scottish actor (Dr Cameron in "Dr. Finlay's Casebook", Body in Library, Murder Most Foul), born in Aberdeen, Scotland (d. 1988)
Glenn McCarthy, American businessman and oil tycoon known as the "King of the Wildcatters", born in Beaumont, Texas (d. 1988)
Mike Mazurki, Austrian-American actor and professional wrestler (Some Like It Hot, It's a Mad Mad Mad Mad World), born in Tarnopol, Galicia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1990)
Helen Twelvetrees, American actress (State's Attorney, Painted Desert), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1958)
Jan Seidel, Czech composer, born in Nymburk, Kingdom of Bohemia, Austrian Empire (d. 1998)
Jo-Jo Moore, American baseball left fielder (MLB All Star 1934–38, 40; World Series 1933; NY Giants), born in Gause, Texas (d. 2001)
Quentin Crisp [Denis Charles Pratt], British author, actor, and raconteur (The Naked Civil Servant), born in Sutton, England (d. 1999)[1][2]
Zora Arkus-Duntov, Belgian-American automotive engineer, known as "the Father of the Corevette", born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1996)
Louis van Lint, Belgian painter (post-war abstraction), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1986)
David Lichine [Lichtenstein], Russian-American dancer and choreographer (Make Mine Music), born in Rostov-on-Don, Russia (d. 1972)
Burne Hogarth, American strip-cartoon artist (Tarzan), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1996)
Louise Bourgeois, French American artist (Femme Maison), born in Paris, France (d. 2010)
Don McRae, New Zealand cricket medium pace bowler (1 Test; Canterbury CA) and soccer goalkeeper (1 cap; Nomads United FC), born in Christchurch, New Zealand (d. 1986)
Herman W. Filarski, Dutch bridge player, journalist and founder of the International Bridge Press Association, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1982)[1]
Leighton Noble [Faye Leighton Jepson], American big band singer, bandleader, and TV host, born in Pasadena, California (d. 1994)
Werner Fussan, German composer (Heiteres Aquarium) and educator, born in Plauen, Germany (d. 1986)
Willian Noel Moffet, Irish architect, born in Cork, Ireland (d. 1994)
Henri Nannen, German journalist (Der Stern), born in Emden, Germany (d. 1996)
Jonathan "Candy" Candido, American actor and radio performer (Peter Pan; The Great Mouse Detective), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1999)
Tony Martin [Alvin Morris], American singer ("It's a Blue World; "To Each His Own"), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2012)
